1: shadow Shout for joy to God, all the earth; The universal shout of joy directed at God reaches its fulfillment in the praise of the Lamb: Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing! (Revelation 5:12). All the earth's joy — the eschatological shout — is concentrated in the risen Christ.
2: shadow sing the glory of his name; give to him glorious praise! The glorious name sung by all the earth is the name above every name (Philippians 2:9-11). Paul commands: in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him (Colossians 3:17). The glorious praise is Christ-centered praise — the Father glorified in the Son.
3: shadow Say to God, How awesome are your deeds! So great is your power that your enemies come cringing to you. The awesome deed of the cross and resurrection: enemies who come cringing — in Paul's framework, every power and principality prostrating before the name of Christ (Philippians 2:10), and ultimately he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet (1 Corinthians 15:25).
4: shadow All the earth worships you and sings praises to you; they sing praises to your name. The universal worship anticipated here is the worship of the Lamb in Revelation (Revelation 5:13 — every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea). Christ is the one to whom all the earth's praise is ultimately directed (John 5:23 — that all may honor the Son, just as they honor the Father).
5: shadow Come and see what God has done: he is awesome in his deeds toward the children of man. The angel's invitation: Come, see the place where he lay (Matthew 28:6). The awesome deed toward humanity is the resurrection — the decisive act by which God demonstrates his power over death for all who trust in Christ (1 Corinthians 15:20-22).
6: type He turned the sea into dry land; they passed through the river on foot. There did we rejoice in him, The Exodus crossing is a type of Christ: Paul identifies the sea-crossing as baptism — all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea (1 Corinthians 10:1-2). The crossing through water into freedom is the pattern Christ fulfills in baptism into his death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4).
7: shadow who rules by his might forever, whose eyes keep watch on the nations — let not the rebellious exalt themselves. Christ rules by might forever from the Father's right hand (Hebrews 1:3; 1 Corinthians 15:25). His eyes as Lord of the nations: his eyes are like a flame of fire (Revelation 1:14; Psalms 2:18) — watching over the nations and addressing each church with divine authority.
8: theme Bless our God, O peoples; let the sound of his praise be heard, The peoples blessing God in Christ: Paul's vision in Romans 15:9-11 of Gentiles glorifying God alongside Israel, citing multiple OT texts showing the universal praise of God. The sound of praise heard among all peoples is the church's mission — the worship of Christ spreading through every tongue.
9: shadow who has kept our soul among the living and has not let our feet slip. Christ keeps those who are his among the living — because I live, you also will live (John 14:19). He who was kept by the Father through death (Acts 2:24 — God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death) now keeps his people so that their feet do not slip into permanent death.
10: shadow For you, O God, have tested us; you have tried us as silver is tried. The testing of Christ in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11), in Gethsemane, and on the cross: he is tried and emerges as refined silver — a lamb without blemish or spot (1 Peter 1:19). The testing of believers is patterned on his: the testing of your faith... more precious than gold (1 Peter 1:7).
11: shadow You brought us into the net; you laid a crushing burden on our backs; The crushing burden: Surely he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows (Isaiah 53:4). Christ takes the burden on himself — the yoke of the cross (Matthew 11:28-30 contrasts his easy yoke with the crushing burden). He enters the net of suffering to carry what no one else can bear.
12: shadow you let men ride over our heads; we went through fire and through water; yet you have brought us out to a place of abundance. Fire and water as trials — 1 Peter 1:7 (faith tested by fire); the place of abundance is the new creation. Christ himself went through the fire of judgment and the water of death to bring his people out to the resurrection abundance (2 Corinthians 4:17 — an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison).
13: shadow I will come into your house with burnt offerings; I will perform my vows to you, Christ is the ultimate burnt offering — who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God (Hebrews 9:14). He performs the vow of the Servant (Isaiah 53:10 — when his soul makes an offering for guilt), entering the house of God not with animal sacrifice but with his own blood (Hebrews 9:12).
14: shadow that which my lips uttered and my mouth promised when I was in trouble. The vow made in trouble — Gethsemane's not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42) is the vow Christ keeps. He promised obedience to the Father through the cup of suffering and delivered it fully on the cross, the faithful keeper of every promise made in the hour of trouble.
15: shadow I will offer to you burnt offerings of fattened animals, with the smoke of the sacrifice of rams; I will make an offering of bulls and goats. The sacrificial system this verse celebrates is fulfilled and ended in Christ: it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins (Hebrews 10:4). Christ's single offering perfects what the repeated offerings of rams and bulls could only shadow (Hebrews 10:10-14).
16: theme Come and hear, all you who fear God, and I will tell what he has done for my soul. The personal testimony of what God has done is the shape of Christian witness: what we have seen and heard we proclaim also to you (1 John 1:3). Christ himself declares the Father's name to his brothers (Hebrews 2:12) — the testimony of what God has done for his soul is the resurrection proclamation.
17: shadow I cried to him with my mouth, and high praise was on my tongue. Christ's cry to the Father from the cross (Matthew 27:46; Luke 23:46) and the praise that followed — the resurrection doxology. He who cried in abandonment also cried in trust; both cries are heard; the praise on his tongue becomes the church's song (Hebrews 13:15).
18: shadow If I had cherished iniquity in my heart, the Lord would not have listened. Christ cherished no iniquity — which of you convicts me of sin? (John 8:46); in him there is no sin (1 John 3:5). Therefore the Lord listened: Hebrews 5:7 — he was heard because of his reverence. His sinless heart is the ground of his efficacious prayer and perfect atonement.
19: shadow But truly God has listened; he has attended to the voice of my prayer. God listened to Christ in Gethsemane — not by removing the cup but by sustaining him through it and raising him from the dead. The ultimate answer to Christ's prayer is the resurrection: God attended to the voice of his prayer and answered with the power that brings life from death (Hebrews 5:7).
20: revelation of God Blessed be God, because he has not rejected my prayer or removed his steadfast love from me! The apparent rejection of the cross — My God, why have you forsaken me? — is overturned by this blessing: God did not ultimately reject his prayer or remove his steadfast love. The resurrection is the proof that divine love was present through every moment of the Passion, never withdrawn, always keeping (Romans 8:38-39).
